concepts.title: Core Tactics — Venue Selection, Talk Craft, and Owning the Aftermath

pretest.prompt: Two founders give the same kind of conference talk in the same year. One produces 200+ inbound leads, three podcast invitations, and a partnership conversation that becomes a $400k deal. The other produces a polite round of applause and nothing measurable. Before reading: what specific tactics — in venue selection, talk design, on-stage delivery, and post-talk distribution — separate them?
pretest.modelAnswer: Five tactical levers. (1) Venue selection — picking conferences and podcasts where your ICP actually attends or listens, not the most prestigious-sounding ones. (2) Talk topic and submission — proposing a talk that's specific, novel, and slightly provocative, not generic 'how to grow your startup' material. (3) Talk craft — opening with a story or a counterintuitive claim; building a single load-bearing argument; using concrete examples; ending with a specific hand-off (URL, book, framework). (4) On-stage delivery — practicing until the talk is internalized, not memorized; managing energy and pacing; staying available afterward for one-on-one conversations. (5) Aftermath ownership — publishing the talk on YouTube the same week, transcribing it into a blog post, cutting clips for social, sending it to your email list, sharing it with the conference organizer for their distribution. The aftermath is where 80% of the channel's compounding value lives. The losing founder picks venues by prestige, gives a generic talk, doesn't practice, leaves immediately after, and never publishes anything from the talk afterward. The winning founder picks venues by ICP density, brings novel substance, rehearses meticulously, stays for the whole conference, and treats the talk as a reusable asset that gets distributed for the next 18 months.

lesson.markdown:
## The five tactical levers

Five specific choices determine whether a speaking engagement compounds or evaporates. Each is a forcing function — getting any one wrong dramatically reduces the channel's ROI.

### Lever 1 — Venue selection by ICP, not prestige

The biggest first-time mistake is picking conferences by name recognition rather than by ICP density. Web Summit and SXSW are famous; LeadDev and Money20/20 are less famous; for a B2B founder selling to engineering leaders or fintech executives, the latter two are dramatically better venues.

Good selection criteria:

- **ICP density.** What fraction of attendees actually fit your ICP? A 1,200-person LeadDev with 800 engineering leaders is better than a 30,000-person Web Summit with 200 engineering leaders.
- **Recording and distribution.** Does the conference post talks on YouTube? On a public podcast feed? With good audio? A talk that lives only as a 45-minute experience is worth a fraction of a talk that gets 50,000 YouTube views over 18 months.
- **Track and slot quality.** A breakout-room talk at 2pm on day three reaches a tenth of the audience of a keynote slot. Negotiate slot quality before accepting.
- **Audience travel intent.** Conferences attendees fly to are higher-signal than free local meetups; people who paid to be there are more likely to act on what they hear.
- **Speaker history.** Who has spoken at this venue before? If past speakers were respected practitioners in your space, the venue is right; if past speakers were generic motivational figures, it isn't.

For podcasts: same logic. The shows your ICP actually listens to may be smaller than the famous ones.

### Lever 2 — Talk topic that earns a slot AND draws an audience

Conferences accept talks that are (a) specific, (b) novel, (c) actionable, and (d) align with the conference's brand. Generic 'lessons learned scaling our startup' submissions get rejected; specific 'how we cut our continuous deployment failure rate from 6% to 0.4% by instrumenting staged rollouts' submissions get accepted.

Good talk topics share three properties:

- **Specific enough to teach something.** The audience walks away with a concrete idea or framework.
- **Novel enough to be worth attending.** The talk says something not already in the audience's collective knowledge.
- **Slightly provocative or counterintuitive.** Not contrarian for its own sake, but with a thesis that makes the audience think 'huh, I want to hear how they argue this.'

A useful test: would your talk get retweeted with a specific quote? If your talk has no quotable load-bearing claim, it won't propagate.

### Lever 3 — Talk craft: structure, story, and hand-off

Great talks share a structural pattern:

- **Opening hook.** A story, a counterintuitive claim, or a vivid example in the first 90 seconds. Establishes attention.
- **Single load-bearing argument.** One thing the talk is trying to convince you of. If you can't state the argument in one sentence, the talk doesn't have one.
- **Concrete examples.** Specific numbers, specific cases, specific characters. Abstract talks slide off; concrete talks stick.
- **Mid-talk friction.** A point where you challenge the audience's prior assumptions. Without friction, the talk is forgettable.
- **Memorable framework or coined term.** Gives the audience something to take home and reference. Patrick McKenzie's 'salary negotiation as a forcing function'; Reid Hoffman's 'blitzscaling.'
- **Specific hand-off.** A URL, a downloadable resource, a book recommendation, a way to follow up. Vague 'visit our website' fails; specific 'go to runloop.com/staged-rollouts for the full case study' works.

What to avoid: dense slides, more than one big idea per talk, generic stock photos, ending with 'thank you and we're hiring.'

### Lever 4 — On-stage delivery and practice

The difference between a talk that lands and a talk that flops is largely practice. Good practice routine:

- Run the talk out loud at full length 4–8 times before the event.
- Record at least one practice run; watch it; cut anything boring.
- Practice with a small live audience (5–10 colleagues or peers) and listen for where attention drops.
- Memorize transitions, not whole sentences. The talk should feel internalized, not recited.
- Plan energy and pacing — the talk should have at least three deliberate energy peaks.

Day-of: arrive early, test the AV, meet the moderator, scan the room. After the talk: stay for the whole conference. The five hours of one-on-one conversations after a good talk often produce more pipeline than the talk itself.

For podcasts: same principles. Prepare 3–5 specific stories you want to tell. Practice the answer to 'tell me about your background' until it's tight. Have a specific call to action ready. Use a quality microphone (Shure SM7B or equivalent — bad audio kills downloads).

### Lever 5 — Aftermath ownership

The aftermath is where 80% of the channel's compounding value lives. Most founders give the talk and stop. Winners treat the recording as a reusable asset:

- **Same week:** confirm the conference has uploaded the recording. If they haven't, ask when. Get the raw video file when possible.
- **Within 2 weeks:** publish the talk on your YouTube channel (with permission) and your blog as an embedded video plus transcribed write-up.
- **Cut clips for social.** 60-second to 3-minute clips of the talk's strongest moments, posted on LinkedIn, Twitter, TikTok if relevant. Each clip can drive its own audience.
- **Email the talk to your list.** Even a small email list amplifies authority lift; a 5,000-person list seeing 'I gave the keynote at LeadDev' beats no follow-up.
- **Use the talk in sales conversations.** A 30-minute talk recording sent to a prospect is dramatically more compelling than a sales deck.
- **Reuse for podcast pitches.** A polished talk recording is your best asset for getting podcast invitations. 'Here's me on stage at LeadDev — would your audience be interested in a similar conversation?'
- **Republish in other formats.** Convert to written essay, to a podcast episode of your own, to a workshop, to a chapter of a book.

A single 45-minute talk, distributed well, can drive inbound for 18 months. The same talk given and forgotten dies in 48 hours.

## What founders systematically get wrong

Three common errors:

1. **Picking venues by prestige instead of ICP fit.** SXSW sounds great; for B2B founders, it usually isn't.
2. **Treating the talk as a one-time event.** The talk is the seed; aftermath is where the channel actually compounds.
3. **Pitching from the stage.** A talk that turns into a product demo immediately destroys the authority lift the channel is supposed to produce.

workedExample.setup: Marcus (Runloop, the continuous-delivery B2B SaaS from the definition concept) has been accepted to give a 30-minute talk at LeadDev London. ICP-perfect audience, recorded for YouTube, well-respected venue. He has 8 weeks until the talk and is planning the full tactical execution.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Topic and submission (already done — accepted talk).** Title: 'How we cut continuous deployment failure rate from 6% to 0.4% by instrumenting staged rollouts.' Specific, novel-ish, technical, ICP-targeted. Conference accepted it because (a) it's specific (not 'lessons in DevOps'), (b) it's data-backed, (c) it teaches something the audience can apply.

**Weeks 8–6 — Talk craft.**
- Marcus drafts the structural skeleton: opening hook (a 30-second story about a botched deployment that took down a customer), single load-bearing argument ('staged rollouts work because they reduce blast radius asymmetrically'), 3 concrete case studies from real customers (anonymized), mid-talk friction point ('most CD playbooks get the math backwards'), coined framework ('the rollout-cost asymmetry'), specific hand-off ('runloop.com/staged-rollouts has the full math model').
- He tests the structure with three engineering leader friends; collects feedback on where attention dropped.
- He cuts 8 minutes from his draft; the talk works better at 22 minutes than 30.

**Weeks 6–4 — Slide deck and visual design.**
- Slides are minimal: one idea per slide, large text, occasional charts. No dense bullet lists.
- Each case study has its own anonymized 'customer X' visual identity.
- Removes all logo-heavy slides except one at the start and one at the end.

**Weeks 4–2 — Practice cycle.**
- Marcus runs the talk out loud 6 times in his living room.
- Records run #4; watches it; identifies 3 boring sections; cuts or restructures them.
- Runs the talk live for 8 colleagues; gets feedback on energy and pacing.
- Practices the opening 90 seconds 20+ times until it lands without notes.

**Week 2 — Pre-event work.**
- Confirms with conference: AV setup, slide ratio, mic type, recording quality.
- Prepares a short 'bio + one-liner' for the moderator.
- Drafts and prepares 3 social media announcements: pre-event, day-of, post-event.
- Pre-publishes the runloop.com/staged-rollouts landing page so it's ready for traffic.
- Schedules a one-day-buffer trip — arrives a day early, stays a day after.

**Day-of execution.**
- Arrives 90 minutes early; tests AV; meets the moderator; greets early attendees.
- Talk lands well: opening story gets laughs, mid-talk friction creates audible reaction, framework gets photographed by attendees with phones, hand-off URL gets noted.
- After the talk: stays in the speaker hallway for 90 minutes answering questions one-on-one.
- Attends the rest of the conference; meets 30+ people; has 12 substantive conversations including 4 with named-prospect VPs Eng.

**Days 1–14 — Aftermath ownership.**
- Day 2: posts a thank-you tweet with key takeaway and the framework name.
- Day 4: confirms with conference that recording will be posted in 3 weeks. Requests raw file in case he wants to publish earlier on his own channel.
- Day 7: writes a 1,500-word blog post version of the talk on the runloop.com blog, tagged for SEO ('staged rollouts continuous deployment').
- Day 10: emails Runloop's 4,200-person email list with the blog post and a video preview.
- Day 14: confirms post on conference's YouTube; embeds it on the runloop.com blog post.

**Weeks 3–8 post-event — Distribution amplification.**
- Week 3: posts the YouTube link on LinkedIn with a 250-word commentary; gets 14k impressions, 47 comments, 8 inbound DMs from VPs Eng.
- Week 4: cuts a 90-second highlight clip; posts on Twitter; goes mildly viral in the DevOps community.
- Week 5: a podcast host (Software Engineering Daily) DMs Marcus asking if he'd come on the show to discuss the talk. Yes.
- Week 6: another podcast invitation. Yes.
- Week 8: SaaSstr's content team asks Marcus to give a similar talk at their event. Yes.

**12-month outcome.**
- YouTube views: 38,000.
- Inbound leads citing the talk or framework: 73 over 12 months.
- Pipeline directly traceable to the talk: $620k.
- Closed-won attributable: 4 customers × $42k ACV = $168k first-year revenue, $420k 2-year LTV.
- Podcast invitations stemming from the talk: 6.
- Speaking invitations stemming from the talk: 4.
- Bonus: the 'staged rollouts' framework name gets cited by analysts and competitors; it becomes Runloop's signature concept.
- ROI on the original talk effort (~80 hours of Marcus's time + $4,000 travel): the channel produced $420k of LTV, plus ongoing inbound that will continue for 12+ months. The compounding asset metric is what justifies the investment.

**What this example illustrates about the tactics.**
- Venue picked by ICP density (LeadDev, not SXSW).
- Topic was specific, novel, and quotable.
- Talk craft followed the hook → argument → examples → friction → framework → hand-off pattern.
- Practice was meticulous; delivery was internalized.
- Aftermath was treated as the main event, not the talk itself.

**What would have killed it.** Generic topic ('lessons learned scaling Runloop') — likely rejected by LeadDev and forgettable if accepted. Dense slides — attention dies in slide three. No practice — talk runs over and crashes. Leaving immediately after — none of the 12 substantive in-person conversations happen. No aftermath — talk dies on day two; no YouTube views, no follow-on podcast invites, no compounding asset. Each lever, executed poorly, would have eliminated 70%+ of the channel's value.

contrast.explanation: PR pitching tactics (Ch 7) and speaking-engagement tactics share the practice of pitching gatekeepers (journalists for press, conference organizers and podcast hosts for stages), but the structure differs. PR pitches sell a story to a journalist who writes about you; the journalist controls the framing and the audience trusts the journalist's editorial voice. Speaking pitches sell a talk to a conference organizer who selects you; you control the framing and the audience trusts the venue's curation as a credibility signal. PR pitches reward news hooks and journalist-friendly angles. Speaking pitches reward novel substance, ICP-fit topic, and a quotable thesis. Both compound — PR mentions accumulate over years; talk recordings accumulate over years — but PR's compounding lives on third-party sites, while speaking's compounding lives partly on third-party sites (conference YouTube) and partly on your own (your blog, your republished video). Rule of thumb: if a journalist writes about you, it's PR; if you stand on a stage or sit in a guest chair, it's speaking. They can support each other (a great talk gets press coverage, leading to more talks), but the tactics are distinct.

flashcards.front: Name the five tactical levers that determine whether a speaking engagement compounds.

(3) Talk craft — hook, single argument, concrete examples, friction, framework, hand-off. (4) On-stage delivery and practice. (5) Aftermath ownership — distribution, clips, blog post, email, sales reuse, podcast pitches.

flashcards.front: Why does aftermath ownership account for 80% of the channel's compounding value?
flashcards.back: A live talk is a one-time delivery; the recording, written version, and distributed clips compound for 18+ months. A 45-minute talk on YouTube with 30,000 views drives inbound continuously. Email distribution amplifies authority. Clips on social drive new audiences. Sales teams use the recording to warm prospects. Without aftermath, the talk dies in 48 hours.

flashcards.front: Describe the structural pattern of a great talk.
flashcards.back: (1) Opening hook — story or counterintuitive claim in first 90 seconds. (2) Single load-bearing argument — one sentence summary. (3) Concrete examples — specific numbers, cases, characters. (4) Mid-talk friction — challenge audience priors. (5) Memorable framework or coined term. (6) Specific hand-off — URL, resource, way to follow up. Avoid: dense slides, multiple big ideas, generic stock photos, 'thank you and we're hiring' endings.

flashcards.front: Venue selection trap: founders often pick by ______ instead of by ______ ______. SXSW sounds great; for B2B founders selling to engineering leaders, ______ is dramatically better. The audience your ICP actually attends matters more than the audience your investors recognize.
flashcards.back: prestige ; ICP density ; LeadDev (or similar specialized venue)

flashcards.front: A founder is told 'pitch your product from the stage — that's why you're paying for the slot.' Why is this advice destructive?
flashcards.back: The channel produces authority transfer, which depends on the audience trusting the speaker as an expert, not perceiving them as a salesperson. A pitch-from-stage talk immediately collapses that trust; the audience tunes out within minutes; reviews of the talk are negative; the speaker doesn't get invited back; the recording (if posted) is unwatchable. The right pattern: deliver substance from the stage; let pipeline emerge from the credibility, not from explicit pitching. Patrick McKenzie's whole speaking career is built on this principle.

scenarios.setup: A founder gave their first conference talk last week — 200 attendees, generally positive feedback. They posted a thank-you tweet and have not done anything else with the recording. They're asking 'why hasn't this produced inbound yet?'
scenarios.question: Walk through the missing aftermath plays they should execute now.
scenarios.modelAnswer: The talk produced authority lift among 200 live attendees but failed to capture the compounding value because the aftermath wasn't executed. Specific plays they should run NOW (within 2 weeks of the talk): (1) Confirm the conference posted the recording on YouTube. If yes, get the link and embed it on their site. If not, request the raw file and post on their own YouTube channel with the conference's permission. (2) Write a 1,500-word blog post version of the talk's main argument. Title it for SEO around the topic. Embed the video. Publish on the company blog. (3) Email the talk + blog post to their full email list. Even a small list amplifies authority lift. (4) Cut 2–3 short clips (60–180 seconds) of the talk's strongest moments. Post on LinkedIn and Twitter with commentary. Each clip can drive its own audience. (5) Send the recording to 5–10 high-priority prospects in pipeline with a personal note ('this might be relevant to what we discussed'). (6) Pitch 3–5 podcasts whose audiences would care about the talk's topic. The recording is the best evidence that the founder can deliver well. (7) Add a mention of the talk to all sales collateral going forward; sales teams reference it in conversations. Why this matters: 200 live attendees is a small audience compared to a YouTube video over 18 months (5,000–50,000 views typical for a good B2B talk on a relevant channel). Aftermath multiplies reach 25–250x. Without it, the founder captured maybe 15% of the channel's value. Time investment for these plays: 6–10 hours total. Expected lift: 5–15x the inbound the talk has produced so far. Meta-lesson: live delivery is the seed; aftermath is the harvest. Founders who skip aftermath are leaving 80% of the channel's value unrealized.

scenarios.setup: A founder of a B2B vertical SaaS has been accepted to speak at three conferences in the next quarter: a 50,000-person flagship cross-industry tech conference, a 1,500-person ICP-perfect vertical event, and a 150-person regional meetup. They have time to prepare for all three but want to invest unequally.
scenarios.question: How should they prioritize prep effort across the three?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Counter-intuitive recommendation: invest the MOST prep effort in the 1,500-person ICP-perfect vertical event, second-most in the regional meetup, least in the flagship cross-industry conference. Reasoning: (1) ICP density determines actionable inbound. The 1,500-person vertical event has perhaps 1,000 ICP-fit attendees; the 50,000-person flagship has perhaps 200 ICP-fit attendees lost in noise. The vertical event will produce 5–10x more pipeline-attributable inbound. (2) Recording distribution favors smaller specialized events. Vertical conferences post talks on their YouTube channels where their target audience subscribes; cross-industry mega-conferences post talks but they get buried under 200+ other talks. (3) Speaker quality at vertical events. Past speakers at the vertical event are known practitioners; the credibility signal of being included is real to ICP. The flagship's signal is generic ('they had a slot at TechCrunch Disrupt'). (4) Audience action probability. Vertical-event attendees came specifically to learn from peers; they're 3–5x more likely to act on what they hear. Flagship attendees are often there for general inspiration; action probability is lower. (5) Regional meetup is small but high-trust; 150 ICP-adjacent people in a room with the founder for 2 hours often produces 2–5 strong conversations and 1–2 deal-relevant relationships. Specific allocation: vertical event — 80 hours prep (talk crafted carefully, slides polished, practice 8x, full aftermath plan). Regional meetup — 20 hours prep (existing material adapted, 3 practice runs, in-person follow-up plan). Flagship — 15 hours prep (talk reused from the vertical event with minor tweaks, light practice, minimal aftermath investment beyond the conference's own recording). Why not just skip the flagship? Reputation lift exists (it's still a name on a CV), and the YouTube recording will produce some long-tail inbound. But the prep cost shouldn't be high relative to the realistic ROI. Meta-lesson: prep effort should scale with pipeline opportunity, not with venue prestige. Most founders invest the opposite way and underperform.

scenarios.setup: A founder with strong product expertise but weak public speaking skills is being told 'just give it a try' and book 10 conferences this year. Their first talk last quarter was technically substantive but visibly nervous, with weak energy and rambling structure. Reviews were lukewarm.
scenarios.question: What's the right path forward — keep going, take a different approach, or skip the channel?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Don't skip the channel — but don't book 10 conferences either. The intermediate path: build speaking capability deliberately before scaling. Three reasons not to skip: (1) The product expertise is real and translates to authority IF delivered well. (2) Speaking capability is learnable; many strong speakers were nervous at first. (3) The channel has high compounding potential for B2B with ICP density at conferences — abandoning it forfeits that. Three reasons not to scale to 10 conferences immediately: (1) 10 mediocre talks produce negative brand association; each weak talk is a public document of weakness, especially if recorded. (2) Founder time is high-cost; investing 80 hours per talk × 10 talks = 800 hours per year is a major commitment for marginal-quality output. (3) Each weak talk reduces the chance of being invited back to better venues. Right path forward: (a) Hire a presentation coach for 6–10 sessions over 3 months. Cost: $3,000–$8,000. Focus on opening, energy, pacing, structure, slide design. Specific, learnable skills. (b) Practice with low-stakes venues first. 3–5 small podcast appearances or 30–50 person meetup talks. Audio-only podcasts are particularly good early venues — no body language pressure, can be edited, lower-stakes. (c) Record practice talks on Loom; watch them; iterate. The founder needs reps in low-stakes contexts before high-stakes ones. (d) Work with one specific talk (not 10 different topics). Get one talk to 'great' state through repetition; then translate the skills to other topics. Patrick McKenzie famously rehearsed his salary negotiation talk dozens of times before it became his signature. (e) Only after coaching + 5+ small reps + one polished signature talk: book 2–3 mid-tier conferences. Then evaluate. Year 2 might support 5–7 talks; year 3 might support 10. Skipping is the wrong answer because abandoning a structurally good channel for a learnable problem is short-sighted. Scaling immediately is the wrong answer because 10 weak talks damage more than they help. Deliberate capability-building is the right answer.
rubric: Rejects both 'skip channel' and 'scale to 10' framings

pretest.prompt: Before you commit to building speaking engagements as a core channel — investing 80–200 hours per year of founder time — how do you cheaply test whether it'll work for your startup, and identify whether YOU specifically can deliver well enough for the channel to compound?
pretest.modelAnswer: Three-tier approach. Tier 1 (1 week, $0–$500): self-test — record yourself on Loom giving a 15-minute version of a potential talk. Watch it. Get feedback from 5 trusted peers. Honest assessment of (a) whether you have novel substance, (b) whether you can deliver well enough, (c) whether you enjoy doing it. Tier 2 (4–8 weeks, $0–$2,000): low-stakes reps — guest on 2–3 small podcasts (audio-only, lower-stakes), give 1–2 small meetup talks (30–80 attendees), get genuine reaction data. Tier 3 (6–12 months, $5,000–$15,000): mid-tier commitment — apply to and execute 2–3 mid-tier conference talks, build aftermath playbook, measure inbound and pipeline attribution over 9 months. Kill criteria: Tier 1 — substance is generic OR delivery is unfixable. Tier 2 — feedback is consistently lukewarm AND no follow-on invitations emerge. Tier 3 — talks produce no measurable inbound, no second-tier venues invite you back, no podcast hosts re-invite you. Anti-pattern: skipping Tier 1 and 2 to commit to a flagship keynote first.

lesson.markdown:
## Why cheap testing matters here specifically

Speaking engagements have a unique testing challenge: the channel's success depends on the founder's personal capability, not just market fit. You can't outsource the delivery the way you can outsource ad creative or content writing. So you're not just testing whether your audience cares — you're also testing whether YOU can deliver well enough to make the channel work.

This means the testing has two axes: (a) is there a real audience for your message? (b) can you actually deliver to that audience? Both must pass.

Unlike paid ads (killable in a day) or content (salvageable as written assets), failed speaking engagements are public — a bad talk has a recording that lives forever, a flopped podcast appearance is on the internet permanently. Testing carefully reduces public-failure cost.

## Tier 1 — Self-test (1 week, $0–$500)

The lowest-stakes test. Before applying to any conference or pitching any podcast, run a self-test:

- **Record a 15-minute version of your candidate talk.** Use Loom or a similar tool. Just you, your slides or talking points, and the recording.
- **Watch the recording end-to-end.** Honestly assess: is this interesting? Would you sit through this if you didn't have to? Does the substance hold up?
- **Show it to 5 trusted peers.** Mix of customer-side people, founders, and people who'd be in your target audience. Ask for blunt feedback on (a) substance — is the idea novel and useful, (b) delivery — is the energy, pacing, and structure working, (c) memorable moments — what stuck, what didn't.

What you learn:

- **Substance test.** Is your message actually novel? Many founders discover their 'expert message' is generic when stated out loud. If 5 peers say 'this is the same stuff everyone is saying,' the topic isn't ready.
- **Delivery test.** Are you watchable? Founders sometimes discover they have unfixable issues (severe nervousness, monotone, unclear thinking under pressure) that need addressing before any public appearance.
- **Enjoyment test.** Do you actually want to do this? Speaking is a long-game commitment; founders who hate doing it usually quit before it compounds.

Kill criteria for Tier 1:
- The substance is generic and you can't differentiate it.
- The delivery is unfixable (after honest peer feedback) and you don't want to invest in coaching.
- You hate the process.

If any kill criterion triggers, address it before continuing — get a coach, sharpen your message, or pick a different channel.

## Tier 2 — Low-stakes reps (4–8 weeks, $0–$2,000)

If Tier 1 passes, get real-audience reps in low-stakes contexts:

- **2–3 small podcast appearances.** Audio-only, smaller shows in your niche. Audio is forgiving (no body language pressure), edits can polish weak moments, the bar to be invited is low. Pitch shows yourself with a clear topic and your Loom recording as proof of capability.
- **1–2 small in-person talks.** Local meetups, internal conferences, brown-bag events at companies whose teams overlap with your ICP. 30–80 person rooms.
- **Optional: 1 short workshop or webinar.** Online format with structured Q&A.

What to measure:

- **Audience reaction.** Did people stay engaged? Did they ask substantive questions? Did anyone reach out afterward?
- **Follow-on invitations.** Did any host or organizer invite you back, or refer you to another venue? This is the strongest signal that you have channel-fit.
- **Recording quality.** Is the recording you can publish actually shareable, or do you cringe watching it?
- **Inbound from the appearances.** Did any pipeline-relevant inbound emerge, even small (newsletter signups, demo requests, LinkedIn connection requests)?

Kill criteria for Tier 2:
- Audience reactions are consistently lukewarm.
- No follow-on invitations emerge across 3+ appearances.
- Recordings aren't shareable (you don't want to publish them).
- No measurable inbound from any appearance.

If Tier 2 passes, you have evidence that you can deliver and that there's an audience. Move to Tier 3.

## Tier 3 — Mid-tier conference commitment (6–12 months, $5,000–$15,000)

If Tier 2 succeeded, scale to mid-tier conferences and build the aftermath playbook:

- **Apply to and execute 2–3 mid-tier conference talks.** Specialized vertical events, regional industry conferences, or mid-size general events. 1,000–3,000 attendee range.
- **Develop the aftermath playbook.** YouTube uploads, blog posts, social clips, email distribution, sales reuse — all standardized so each talk gets full distribution.
- **Track inbound and pipeline.** Tag every inbound that mentions a talk; track these contacts through pipeline; measure conversion at 6, 9, and 12 months.

Kill criteria for Tier 3:
- Talks produce no measurable inbound after 9 months.
- No second-tier venues invite you back.
- No podcast hosts re-invite you for follow-up appearances.
- Pipeline traceable to talks is <2x the time-cost of preparation and aftermath.

If Tier 3 passes, the channel is ready to scale. Year 2 can support 5–8 talks; year 3 can support 10–15 with a mature aftermath playbook.

## Anti-patterns to avoid

- **Skipping straight to a flagship keynote.** Most expensive form of testing. Flopping at TechCrunch Disrupt is a public document of weakness.
- **Treating the first talk as the whole channel test.** A single talk is one rep; the channel rewards 5+ reps before signal stabilizes.
- **Measuring only short-term inbound.** Speaking ROI shows up over 6–18 months as the recordings accumulate views.
- **Hiring a speech coach without doing the substance work first.** Coaching polishes delivery but can't manufacture novel substance.
- **Confusing 'enjoyed myself' with 'channel works.'** A founder might genuinely enjoy speaking but produce no inbound; the test is the channel's actual output, not the founder's experience.

## The pre-commitment checklist

Before investing 80+ hours per year in speaking engagements as a core channel:

1. Have you self-tested with a Loom recording and 5-peer feedback?
2. Have you executed 3+ low-stakes reps (small podcasts + meetups)?
3. Have you completed 2+ mid-tier conference talks with measurable outcomes?
4. Do you have an aftermath playbook (YouTube, blog, social, email, sales reuse) standardized?
5. Do you have a way to attribute inbound back to specific talks?
6. Are you committed to multi-year investment (the channel compounds; one year doesn't tell the story)?

If any answer is 'no,' defer the major commitment.

workedExample.setup: Sara runs a $30k ACV vertical SaaS for accounting firm operations. Strong technical knowledge, somewhat introverted, has never given a public talk. Her advisor suggests speaking engagements as a candidate channel. She has $8k budget for testing this channel and 9 months.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Tier 1 — Self-test (week 1, $0).**

Sara records a 15-minute Loom of herself giving a candidate talk: 'How accounting firms are restructuring operations for the AI era.' She watches it back. Honest assessment: substance is good (specific to her vertical, novel angle), delivery is okay (energy is low but ideas come through), structure is wandering.

She sends the Loom to 5 peers: 2 accounting firm partners (target audience), 1 founder peer, 1 conference organizer she knows, 1 trusted advisor.

Feedback comes back:
- Substance: 'This is interesting and not generic.'
- Delivery: 'Energy is low, but the content carries it.' One peer adds: 'Coachable, not a fundamental issue.'
- Structure: 'You meander in the middle; tighten the second half.'
- Enjoyment: Sara reflects honestly. She doesn't love it, but she doesn't hate it. The intellectual challenge appeals to her.

Tier 1 verdict: PASS with caveats. Substance is real. Delivery is fixable. Enjoyment is moderate. Move to Tier 2 with a delivery improvement plan.

**Investment in delivery improvement: $1,200.** Sara hires a speech coach for 4 sessions focused on energy, pacing, and structure.

**Tier 2 — Low-stakes reps (months 2–4, $1,500).**

*Rep 1: Audio podcast appearance.* Sara pitches herself to a small accounting-industry podcast (5,000 weekly listeners). They invite her on. 45-minute episode. Audio is forgiving; her energy comes through better than on video. Episode receives positive comments. The host invites her back to do a follow-up episode in 6 months.

*Rep 2: Local meetup.* 50-person accounting professionals' regional meetup in her city. 20-minute talk plus Q&A. She uses the structure improvements from coaching. The talk lands well — three attendees come up afterward to ask follow-up questions; one becomes a prospect within 4 weeks.

*Rep 3: Larger podcast.* Pitched to a more prominent vertical podcast (15,000 weekly listeners) using her two prior episodes as proof. They invite her on. 60-minute conversation. The episode generates 11 inbound LinkedIn connection requests over the next 6 weeks; 4 turn into substantive sales conversations.

Tier 2 verdict: PASS. Audience reactions positive; one re-invitation; one new inbound podcast invitation; small but real pipeline emergence. Move to Tier 3.

**Tier 3 — Mid-tier conference (months 5–11, $4,500).**

Sara applies to two mid-tier accounting industry conferences. One accepts her for a 30-minute breakout talk on her topic.

*Conference talk preparation (months 5–7).* Sara invests 60 hours of preparation: refining the talk topic, developing the framework, designing slides, practicing 8 times, doing 3 dry runs with peers, working with her speech coach on energy and pacing.

*Conference talk delivery (month 8).* 200-person breakout room. Talk lands well. 35 people stay after for Q&A and one-on-one conversation. She collects 22 business cards (organic, not solicited).

*Aftermath (months 8–11).* Sara executes the playbook: YouTube post (4,800 views over 90 days), blog post version on her company site, 3 social clips (6,200 LinkedIn impressions on the strongest), email to her 1,800-person list, sales team adds the talk to outreach sequences.

Tier 3 outcomes at month 11:
- Inbound traceable to the talk or aftermath: 31 prospects.
- Pipeline created: $180k.
- Closed-won: 2 customers × $30k = $60k first-year revenue, $90k 2-year LTV.
- Podcast invitations from the talk: 2.
- Speaking invitations from the talk: 1 (a larger conference next year).
- ROI on Tier 3 investment ($4,500 + ~70 hours of Sara's time): channel produced $90k LTV directly, plus a podcast and speaking pipeline that compounds further.

Tier 3 verdict: PASS. Real inbound emerged. Follow-on invitations validate channel-fit. Move to year-2 commitment.

**Year 2 plan.** Sara commits to: 4 mid-tier conference talks, 6 podcast appearances, 1 stretch goal (a flagship keynote at a major industry event). Total investment: ~150 hours of her time + $12,000 in travel, conference fees, coaching follow-up. Expected return: $250k–$500k LTV from the channel based on Year 1 trajectory.

**What this example illustrates.** Tier 1 caught the structure issue early (cheap to fix). Tier 2 gave Sara low-stakes reps to build capability before high-stakes commitments. Tier 3 produced real outcomes that justify scaling. Total investment to channel-validation: $7,200 over 11 months, plus founder time. Counterfactual (skipping Tier 1 and 2): Sara might have committed to a flagship keynote first, given a low-energy meandering talk in front of 1,500 people, produced little inbound, and concluded 'speaking doesn't work for us.' Tiered testing prevented that mis-diagnosis.

contrast.explanation: Testing trade shows (Ch 21) and testing speaking engagements both use a tiered de-risking structure but differ in what's being tested. Trade-show testing tests whether the audience-organizer combination produces qualified pipeline at the booth — it's about external fit (does the organizer's audience match your ICP, does your booth execution work). Speaking-engagement testing tests two things simultaneously: whether the audience cares about your message AND whether you specifically can deliver well enough. The second axis is more personal and harder to outsource. Trade shows can be tested with $1,500–$2,000 at Tier 1 (attend as a guest); speaking can be tested with $0 at Tier 1 (Loom recording self-assessment). Trade shows produce signal in 3–6 months (pipeline conversion); speaking produces signal in 6–18 months (recording views and citation-based inbound). The structural commonality: both reward never skipping straight to the largest bet. The structural difference: speaking requires honest self-assessment of capability, which trade shows do not.

flashcards.front: Describe the three-tier cheap-testing approach for speaking engagements.
flashcards.back: Tier 1 (1 week, $0–$500): self-test — record yourself on Loom, watch it, get feedback from 5 trusted peers on substance, delivery, enjoyment. Tier 2 (4–8 weeks, $0–$2,000): low-stakes reps — 2–3 small podcasts + 1–2 small meetup talks. Tier 3 (6–12 months, $5,000–$15,000): mid-tier conference commitment with full aftermath playbook + 9-month attribution measurement.

flashcards.front: Why does speaking-engagement testing have two axes, unlike most other channels?
flashcards.back: The channel's success depends on the founder's personal capability, not just market fit. So testing has two axes: (a) is there a real audience for your message; (b) can YOU deliver well enough to make the channel work. You can't outsource the delivery the way you can outsource ad creative or content writing — both axes must pass.

flashcards.front: Name the three things to test in Tier 1 (self-test).
flashcards.back: (1) Substance — is your message novel and useful, or generic? (2) Delivery — are you watchable, or are there unfixable issues like severe nervousness or monotone? (3) Enjoyment — do you actually want to do this long-term? Founders who hate the process quit before the channel compounds.

flashcards.front: The strongest signal of channel-fit at Tier 2 is ______-______ ______ — when a host or organizer invites you back or refers you to another venue. This signals capability AND audience fit simultaneously.
flashcards.back: follow-on invitations — without them, even positive audience reactions may be polite rather than genuinely engaged.

flashcards.front: A founder skips Tier 1 and 2 and books a flagship keynote at a 5,000-person tech conference as their first speaking engagement. What's the structural risk?
flashcards.back: Highest-stakes failure context. If the founder isn't ready (substance is generic, delivery is weak, structure wanders), the talk is recorded and lives publicly forever as evidence of weakness. Worse: it actively damages brand because it suggests the company can't deliver clear thinking. Worse still: the founder concludes 'speaking doesn't work' from a single bad rep at the wrong stakes. Tiered testing exists specifically to prevent this — capability-building must precede high-stakes deployment.

scenarios.setup: A founder did Tier 1 self-test. Substance is novel. Delivery feedback was 'nervous and a bit monotone.' Enjoyment is moderate. They're asking: kill the channel, or invest in coaching?
scenarios.question: Walk through the right decision.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Don't kill the channel — delivery weakness is fixable in a way that substance weakness is not. Diagnostic logic: (1) Substance test passed. The novel substance is the rare and valuable part; many founders never have it. (2) Delivery weakness ('nervous, monotone') is in the category of fixable issues. Public speaking is a learnable skill; presentation coaches routinely take introverted technical founders from weak to competent in 4–8 sessions. (3) Enjoyment is moderate. Not love-it-need-it, but not hate. Sufficient for sustained investment if outcomes are strong. (4) Cost-benefit: $2,000–$5,000 for coaching is small relative to the channel's potential ($100k–$500k LTV per year if it works). Recommendation: invest in coaching first, then progress to Tier 2. Specific plan: (a) Hire a presentation coach for 6–8 sessions over 3 months. Focus on energy, pacing, and stage presence — the specific weaknesses identified. Cost: $3,000–$5,000. (b) Re-do the Tier 1 self-test after coaching. Confirm delivery has improved with another peer feedback round. (c) If improved, move to Tier 2. (d) If not improved (rare but possible), consider whether speaking is genuinely fit for this founder OR whether co-founder/team-member should be the speaker instead. Counter-scenario where you'd kill: substance test failed (message is generic) AND delivery is hard to fix. That combination is structural channel-misfit. But novel-substance + fixable-delivery is the most common shape, and the right move is to fix delivery, not abandon the channel. Meta-lesson: substance is the rare ingredient; delivery is the common-but-fixable one. Don't kill a channel because of a fixable problem when the rare ingredient is present.
rubric: Distinguishes substance (rare, hard to fix) from delivery (common, fixable)
Recommends coaching investment with specific cost
Specifies re-test gate after coaching
Considers team-substitution as a backup option
Articulates the 'don't kill for fixable problems' principle
Quantifies coaching cost vs channel potential

scenarios.setup: A founder completed Tier 2 with strong results: 3 podcasts, 1 meetup talk, all positive reactions, 2 follow-on invitations, 8 inbound leads. They want to scale year 1 to 12 mid-tier conferences plus 20 podcasts.
scenarios.question: Is this a reasonable scaling pace?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Too aggressive for year 1 even with strong Tier 2 results. Capacity analysis: (1) Mid-tier conference talk preparation: 60–80 hours per talk done well (includes topic refinement, slide design, practice cycle, on-site execution, aftermath). 12 conferences × 70 hours = 840 hours. That's roughly 40% of a founder's working hours per year just on conference talk prep. (2) Podcast appearances: 4–8 hours each for prep, recording, and aftermath. 20 podcasts × 6 hours = 120 hours. (3) Total time investment: ~960 hours, or 50% of a working year. This dwarfs founder time available for product, sales, hiring, fundraising, customer success. (4) Quality drops with frequency. Talk prep that takes 70 hours done right takes 30 hours rushed; the rushed version produces a forgettable talk and weak aftermath, eroding the channel's compounding value. (5) Even strong Tier 2 results don't predict Tier 3 capacity for 12 conferences. The Tier 2 reps were small-stakes and lower-prep; mid-tier conferences require dramatically more investment per slot. Right scaling: year 1 — 3–4 mid-tier conferences + 6–8 podcasts. Total time: ~280 hours. Sustainable. Allows quality preparation. Builds aftermath playbook. Year 2 — based on year-1 results, scale to 5–7 conferences + 10–12 podcasts if the channel is producing pipeline. Year 3 — 8–10 conferences + 15+ podcasts if year-2 sustains. Why this specific pacing: (a) Each year's results inform whether to scale further. Premature scaling locks in commitments before evidence justifies them. (b) Founder time is the binding constraint, not budget. 12 conferences in year 1 means founder time on product drops dramatically — bad trade for an early-stage company. (c) Aftermath playbook needs iteration. Doing 3–4 conferences in year 1 with great aftermath beats 12 conferences with sloppy aftermath. Recommendation: redirect the over-ambitious year-1 plan to a sustainable schedule, document outcomes carefully, and let evidence justify year-2 expansion.
rubric: Quantifies time investment per talk and per podcast
Identifies founder time as the binding constraint
Predicts quality degradation from rushed prep
Distinguishes Tier 2 reps from Tier 3 commitment requirements
Recommends specific sustainable year-1 pace
Articulates the 'evidence-justified scaling' principle

teachback.prompt: Explain how to test speaking engagements cheaply. Cover: (1) why this channel uniquely tests two axes (audience interest AND personal capability); (2) Tier 1 self-test with the three things to assess; (3) Tier 2 low-stakes reps and what to measure; (4) Tier 3 mid-tier conference commitment with attribution; (5) anti-patterns to avoid including jumping to flagships; (6) the pre-commitment checklist before scaling.

concepts.title: Pitfalls of the Speaking Engagements Channel

pretest.prompt: Speaking engagements have produced lasting authority for some founders (Patrick McKenzie, Des Traynor, Rand Fishkin, Seth Godin) and have produced nothing measurable for thousands of others who give talks every quarter. Before reading: what are the most common ways the channel destroys value, even when the theoretical fit looks right?
pretest.modelAnswer: Eight pitfalls. (1) Pitch-slapping from the stage — turning the talk into a product demo destroys authority transfer. (2) Speaking too early — before message is sharp or product is real, the talk produces credibility damage. (3) Picking venues by prestige instead of ICP fit — SXSW reach is wasted for B2B founders. (4) Skipping aftermath — 80% of compounding value lives in distribution, not delivery. (5) No lead capture — talks that produce inbound need a way to track it back; without attribution, you can't prove ROI. (6) Generic talk topics — 'lessons learned' titles get rejected or ignored. (7) Inadequate practice — winging it produces forgettable talks and visible nervousness. (8) Speaking without follow-up — the warm conversations after a talk produce most of the immediate pipeline; leaving immediately wastes the channel's primary near-term output. Plus (9) over-committing to too many talks per year (quality drops); (10) measuring by activity (talk count) instead of outcomes (inbound and pipeline).

lesson.markdown:
## Pitfall 1 — Pitch-slapping from the stage

The single most damaging mistake. The founder turns the talk into a product demo, fills slides with logos and CTAs, ends with 'visit our booth or schedule a demo.' Audience tunes out within five minutes; the channel's authority transfer collapses.

**Mechanism:** the channel works because the audience trusts the speaker as an expert. When the speaker reveals themselves as a salesperson, that trust evaporates instantly. The audience reads the rest of the talk through a 'they're trying to sell me something' filter; substance becomes invisible.

**Preventive:** explicit no-pitch rule. The talk teaches something — a framework, a case study, a counterintuitive lesson. The product appears, if at all, only as a brief contextual mention. Pipeline emerges from the audience's perception of expertise, not from explicit asks. Patrick McKenzie's career is built on this principle.

## Pitfall 2 — Speaking too early

Founders who speak before their message is sharp or their product is real produce talks that damage credibility instead of building it.

**Mechanism:** the audience can detect when a speaker is improvising substance or when the underlying product doesn't yet support the claims being made. A 'we have learned X about scaling' talk from a 12-customer startup invites skepticism. A 'how we built Y' talk where Y barely exists looks dishonest.

**Preventive:** wait until you have (a) a sharpened message based on real customer signal, (b) a product with results worth claiming, (c) at least 6 months of experience with the topic you'll discuss. Earlier-stage founders should focus on smaller venues and substantive topics they actually own.

## Pitfall 3 — Picking venues by prestige instead of ICP fit

Founders book SXSW, Web Summit, or other famous venues because the names sound impressive on a CV. The audiences are wrong for B2B and most vertical SaaS.

**Mechanism:** prestige doesn't equal ICP density. A 50,000-person flagship with 200 ICP-fit attendees produces less inbound than a 1,500-person specialized event with 1,000 ICP-fit attendees. The famous venue feels good but produces poor outcomes.

**Preventive:** select venues by ICP density, not headline name. Ask 'how many attendees match my ICP?' not 'how prestigious is this conference?' For most B2B founders, mid-tier specialized events outperform flagship general events.

## Pitfall 4 — Skipping aftermath

The founder gives the talk, posts a thank-you tweet, and never publishes the recording, never writes a blog version, never cuts clips, never distributes it.

**Mechanism:** 80% of the channel's compounding value lives in the aftermath. A live talk reaches 200–2,000 people once; a published recording reaches 5,000–50,000+ over 18 months. Without aftermath, the channel produces 20% of its potential value.

**Preventive:** treat the recording as the main asset, not the live delivery. Publish on YouTube within 2 weeks. Write a blog post version. Cut clips for social. Email to your list. Use in sales conversations. Standardize the playbook so every talk gets full distribution.

## Pitfall 5 — No lead capture or attribution

The founder gives talks but has no way to track whether resulting inbound is actually attributable. They eventually conclude 'I can't prove speaking is working' — which is true because they didn't instrument it.

**Mechanism:** without attribution, the channel can't be measured, can't be defended in budget conversations, and can't be optimized. Founders abandon it because the case for it is invisible.

**Preventive:** build attribution into every talk. Use unique URLs for hand-offs (talk-specific landing pages); ask 'how did you hear about us' on signup forms; tag every CRM contact with 'speaking' source plus specific event when known; track the aggregate over 6–18 month windows.

## Pitfall 6 — Generic talk topics

The founder pitches 'lessons learned scaling our startup,' 'building a great product team,' or 'tips for raising your seed round.' The conference rejects the talk; if accepted, the audience tunes out because they've heard the talk a hundred times.

**Mechanism:** generic topics fail two tests — they don't get accepted at quality venues, and they don't produce memorable talks if they are accepted. Specific, novel, slightly counterintuitive topics get accepted and remembered.

**Preventive:** propose talks with (a) specific subject matter (a particular problem you solved with particular numbers), (b) novel angle (something the audience hasn't heard before), (c) a quotable load-bearing claim. The 'how we cut continuous deployment failure rate from 6% to 0.4% by instrumenting staged rollouts' format works; the 'lessons in DevOps' format doesn't.

## Pitfall 7 — Inadequate practice

The founder writes the talk, glances at it once, and walks on stage. Result: nervous delivery, missed beats, missed energy peaks, time overruns.

**Mechanism:** great talks look effortless because they're internalized; under-practiced talks look uncertain. Audience perceives the uncertainty and discounts the substance.

**Preventive:** rehearse 4–8 times out loud at full length. Record at least one practice run; watch it. Practice with a small live audience. Memorize transitions, not whole sentences. Plan deliberate energy peaks. The best speakers practice in proportion to the stakes.

## Pitfall 8 — No follow-up after the talk

The founder gives the talk, walks off stage, leaves the conference immediately. The 5 hours of warm one-on-one conversations that would have followed don't happen.

**Mechanism:** the immediate-pipeline output of the channel comes mostly from post-talk conversations, not from the talk itself. Audience members who were intrigued want to talk; if you're not there, they don't.

**Preventive:** stay for the entire conference. Be visible in hallways, at speaker dinners, in sponsor halls. Make yourself available for one-on-one conversations. Carry business cards or be ready to scan badges. The introvert version: schedule specific 'office hours' slots and announce them at the end of the talk.

## Pitfall 9 — Over-committing to too many talks per year

Founders who succeed with one talk often book 12 the next year, hoping to scale the success. Quality drops; aftermath gets sloppy; the founder burns out.

**Mechanism:** each well-executed talk requires 60–80 hours of preparation plus aftermath. 12 talks × 70 hours = 840 hours. Combined with founder duties, this is unsustainable. Quality erosion produces forgettable talks; the compounding loop reverses.

**Preventive:** scale gradually. Year 1 — 3–4 talks well done. Year 2 — 5–7 if outcomes justify. Year 3 — 8–12 only with dedicated aftermath team support. Quality > quantity.

## Pitfall 10 — Measuring by activity instead of outcomes

The founder reports 'I gave 6 talks this year' as a success metric. But talk count isn't the goal — inbound, pipeline, and brand lift are.

**Mechanism:** activity metrics drive optimization toward visibility, not impact. A founder who gives 12 generic talks looks busy but produces less than a founder who gives 4 great talks with full aftermath.

**Preventive:** measure outcomes — inbound traceable to talks, pipeline created, closed-won attribution over 12 months, brand mentions stemming from the talks, follow-on speaking and podcast invitations. Talk count is an input, not an output.

workedExample.setup: A founder gave 8 conference talks last year. Their year-end report: '8 talks given, ~3,200 total live audience attendance, 15 podcast invitations.' When asked about pipeline and inbound, they say 'hard to say — I haven't tracked it specifically.' They're proposing to scale to 12 talks next year.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Diagnosis across the 10 pitfalls.**

1. **Pitch-slapping** — unknown but possible.
2. **Speaking too early** — unknown.
3. **Venue selection by prestige** — unknown but worth auditing; 8 talks across many venues suggests they may not be selecting carefully.
4. **Skipping aftermath** — likely. A founder who gave 8 talks but can't quantify inbound almost certainly didn't execute aftermath playbooks. Talk recordings probably aren't published on the founder's site, social clips probably weren't cut, etc.
5. **No lead capture/attribution** — confirmed by the founder's own admission ('hard to say').
6. **Generic talk topics** — possible.
7. **Inadequate practice** — possible at scale of 8 talks (probably reused material).
8. **No follow-up** — possible.
9. **Over-commitment** — clearly happening. 8 talks in year 1 + proposing 12 next year suggests activity-driven scaling.
10. **Measuring by activity** — clearly happening. The reported metric is talk count, not outcome.

**Real diagnosis.** The founder is in a classic activity-metrics trap. They're producing visible activity (8 talks, 3,200 attendees, podcast invitations) but can't measure outcomes because they didn't instrument attribution AND didn't execute aftermath. The proposal to scale to 12 talks doubles down on activity without addressing whether the channel is actually producing pipeline.

**Counterfactual estimation.** With proper aftermath and attribution, 8 well-distributed talks could plausibly produce $400k–$1.5M in pipeline-attributable revenue per year for a typical mid-ACV B2B SaaS. Without aftermath: the same 8 talks probably produce $50k–$200k in implicit goodwill that's invisible without attribution. The channel may be working — they have no way to know.

**What to do instead of scaling to 12.**

Step 1 — Pause new talk commitments for 60 days. Do not book any new conferences until the system is fixed.

Step 2 — Build the attribution system. (a) Set up unique landing-page URLs per talk (talk-specific tracking). (b) Add 'how did you hear about us' field to all forms. (c) Tag CRM contacts with talk source where known. (d) Backfill what's possible: review recent inbound, ask sales reps to tag any 'I heard you speak' mentions.

Step 3 — Build the aftermath playbook. (a) Take the best 3 talks from last year; publish recordings on YouTube + the company blog. (b) Cut social clips from each. (c) Email each to the company list. (d) Add to sales sequences. The compounding value of last year's 8 talks is largely unrealized; even partial aftermath now produces real lift.

Step 4 — Re-evaluate next year's talk count. With proper attribution and aftermath, 4–5 well-prepared talks with full distribution will likely produce more pipeline than 12 sloppy talks. The right scale is the one that matches founder capacity for excellent execution + aftermath, which is probably 5–7 per year, not 12.

Step 5 — Define outcome metrics for next year. Inbound contacts traceable to talks. Pipeline created. Closed-won at 6, 9, 12 months. Brand mentions and follow-on invitations. Reports go to leadership with these metrics, not talk counts.

**Meta-lesson.** Activity metrics seduce founders into thinking they're producing value when they're not measuring whether they are. The 8-talks-per-year founder might be doing great work or wasting their time — without attribution, they don't know, which means they can't optimize, which means quality and aftermath drift downward over time. Scaling activity without measuring outcomes is the path to a low-ROI year of speaking commitments. The fix is attribution + aftermath FIRST, then scale based on evidence.

contrast.explanation: Core tactics explain the plays that make the channel compound; pitfalls explain the failure modes that destroy value. They operate within the same channel but on different phases — tactics are forward-looking ('what to do'); pitfalls are pattern-recognition ('what to avoid'). Many practical decisions need both framings simultaneously. The aftermath ownership tactic IS the defense against the skipping-aftermath pitfall. The venue-selection-by-ICP tactic IS the defense against the prestige-over-ICP pitfall. The deliberate-practice tactic IS the defense against the inadequate-practice pitfall. Mastery requires holding both: do the right things AND avoid the wrong ones. A founder who knows the tactics but ignores the pitfalls (e.g., picks venues for ICP density but pitch-slaps from stage) destroys the channel's value despite executing some elements correctly. The two concepts are complementary halves of the same operational discipline.

flashcards.front: Name the eight major pitfalls of the speaking engagements channel.
flashcards.back: (1) Pitch-slapping from the stage. (2) Speaking too early. (3) Venue selection by prestige instead of ICP fit. (4) Skipping aftermath. (5) No lead capture or attribution. (6) Generic talk topics. (7) Inadequate practice. (8) No follow-up after the talk. Plus (9) over-commitment to too many talks; (10) measuring by activity instead of outcomes.

flashcards.front: Why does pitch-slapping from the stage destroy the channel's authority transfer?
flashcards.back: The channel works because the audience trusts the speaker as an expert. When the speaker reveals themselves as a salesperson, that trust evaporates within minutes. The audience reads the rest of the talk through a 'they're trying to sell me something' filter; substance becomes invisible. The recording is also unwatchable, eliminating compounding value.

flashcards.front: What's the 'no lead capture/attribution' pitfall, and how do you prevent it?
flashcards.back: Without attribution, the channel can't be measured, defended in budget conversations, or optimized. Founders abandon it because the case for it is invisible. Prevention: unique tracked URLs per talk, 'how did you hear about us' on forms, CRM tagging with talk source, attribution windows of 6–18 months.

flashcards.front: Skipping aftermath pitfall: ______% of the channel's compounding value lives in distribution, not delivery. A live talk reaches 200–2,000 people once; a published recording reaches ______–______+ over 18 months. Without aftermath, the channel produces ______% of its potential value.
A founder reports 'I gave 12 talks this year' as their year-end success metric. What's wrong with this framing?
flashcards.back: Activity metrics, not outcome metrics. Talk count is an input, not an output. The actual success metrics are inbound traceable to talks, pipeline created, closed-won attribution at 6/9/12 months, brand mentions, and follow-on invitations. A founder who gives 4 great talks with full aftermath produces more than one who gives 12 generic talks without aftermath. Scaling activity without measuring outcomes optimizes for visibility, not impact.

scenarios.setup: A founder is preparing their first conference talk. The marketing team has prepared slides full of customer logos, screenshots of the product, and a 'visit our booth!' CTA at the end. The founder thinks the slides look professional.
scenarios.question: What's wrong, and how should they restructure?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Classic pitch-slap setup. The slides as designed will collapse the channel's value the moment they're presented. Specific damages: (1) Audience perception flips immediately when product screenshots and customer-logo grids appear. The talk reads as a vendor pitch, not an expert delivery. (2) The 'visit our booth!' CTA at the end signals the entire talk was a setup for a sales ask. Trust evaporates retroactively. (3) The recording is unwatchable for compounding distribution; nobody shares a 30-minute product demo. Restructuring: (a) Strip all customer-logo slides except possibly one anonymized 'companies like X have used this approach' reference if relevant to the substance. (b) Replace product screenshots with conceptual diagrams, frameworks, or anonymized case studies. The product can appear briefly as context for the framework but should never be the slide's subject. (c) Eliminate the 'visit our booth' CTA. Replace with a substantive hand-off: a URL where the audience can download a deeper resource, a recommended book, a framework PDF. (d) Restructure the talk around a single load-bearing claim ('staged rollouts work because of asymmetric blast radius') with concrete examples. The talk teaches a lesson; the audience learns something they can apply tomorrow. (e) Brand identity shows up subtly — a footer with the company name, a clean visual identity, but not as the focus. Why this works: the audience trusts experts who teach, not salespeople who pitch. Pipeline emerges from credibility built during the talk, not from explicit asks. The recording becomes shareable because it's substantive content, not a demo. Counter-example to study: any Patrick McKenzie talk on YouTube. Notice how he never pitches; he teaches deep substance, often about topics adjacent to his work, and the audience comes away thinking 'I want to follow this person.' That's the channel working. Meta-lesson: marketing instincts toward 'professional polish with logos and CTAs' actively destroy the speaking channel's value. The right talk feels more like a peer teaching than a vendor selling.
rubric: Identifies pitch-slap setup as core problem
Specifies damage to audience trust and recording shareability
Prescribes specific slide restructuring
Replaces sales CTA with substantive hand-off
References Patrick McKenzie or similar for counterexample
Articulates 'teach not pitch' principle

scenarios.setup: A founder gave 6 conference talks in year 1. Three of them produced 4–8 inbound leads each; the other three produced essentially nothing. The founder concludes 'speaking is unreliable — half my talks were wasted.' They want to cut speaking from year 2.
scenarios.question: Diagnose the conclusion. Is it justified?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Conclusion is wrong. The variance the founder describes is normal for the channel; the right move is to analyze WHY some talks worked and others didn't, not to abandon the channel. Diagnostic questions: (1) What was different about the 3 successful talks? Likely candidates: better venue ICP fit, sharper topic, more polished delivery, better aftermath execution. (2) What was different about the 3 failed talks? Likely candidates: prestige-driven venue selection, generic topic, weak aftermath, no attribution setup. The variance is signal about which conditions produce results, not a verdict on the channel. Real failure modes that may be at play: (a) Venue selection variance — some talks were at ICP-perfect events, others at general events. (b) Aftermath inconsistency — successful talks may have had blog posts and social distribution; failed talks may have had nothing. (c) Topic specificity variance — successful talks may have had specific quotable claims; failed talks may have been generic. (d) Attribution gaps — failed talks may actually have produced inbound that wasn't attributed. Recommended action: (1) Don't cut speaking. Cutting based on variance abandons a channel that's clearly working in some conditions. (2) Audit the 6 talks. Identify what's different between successful and failed. Build a 'talk fit checklist' from the lessons. (3) Year 2: do 4–5 talks that meet the success-pattern criteria from year 1. Skip the venues and topics that match the failure-pattern criteria. Quality over quantity. (4) Build proper attribution for year-2 talks so the next 'failed' talk can be analyzed properly — maybe it actually produced inbound that wasn't tracked. (5) Measure year-2 outcomes against the refined approach; reassess. Why this is right: speaking engagements is a high-variance channel where 30–50% of talks underperform even for skilled practitioners — that's normal. The path to high-ROI use is selecting better venues, sharper topics, and stronger aftermath, not abandoning the channel. Meta-lesson: variance is a signal to refine, not to quit. Cutting a channel because some attempts failed is the same logic as cutting paid ads because some campaigns underperformed — the right move is to analyze and optimize.
rubric: Identifies the conclusion as the wrong response to variance
Names specific candidate causes for the variance
Recommends audit of successful vs failed talks
Builds 'talk fit checklist' from year-1 lessons
Adds attribution for year 2
Articulates 'variance is signal to refine, not quit' principle

scenarios.setup: A founder gives talks with strong substance but consistently rambles, runs over time, and has visible nervous tics. The audience clearly disengages by minute 15 of every 30-minute talk. They've been told 'you'll get better with practice' but 18 talks in, the issue persists.
scenarios.question: What's the right intervention?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Generic 'practice will fix it' advice has failed; structured intervention is needed. Three options, in priority order: (1) Hire a presentation coach for 8–12 sessions. Specific focus areas: structure (rambling = no clear single load-bearing argument), pacing (running over time = no rehearsal of timing), nervousness (visible tics = no exposure-based desensitization plus behavioral techniques). $4k–$8k investment. Most likely to produce improvement. (2) Switch primary delivery to formats that play to strengths. If audio podcasts are easier than on-stage talks (which is common for nervous founders), shift weight to podcast guest appearances. Remove the body language pressure; audio editing fixes pacing issues. Cost: minimal. Trades reach for delivery quality. (3) Have a co-founder or team member become the primary speaker. Some founders are wonderful 1:1 but never become great public speakers despite years of effort — that's just personality fit. If a co-founder or senior team member is a stronger speaker, give them the talks. The founder still benefits from authority transfer (their company is on stage) without the personal-capability constraint. Why 'practice' alone hasn't worked: 18 reps without structured improvement just trains the existing bad habits more deeply. Practice without feedback loops is repetition, not skill development. Coaching provides the structured feedback. Switching format provides an easier modality. Substituting speakers acknowledges that some founders are not natural public speakers regardless of effort. Recommended path: try (1) coaching first — it's the highest-leverage intervention and most founders see meaningful improvement. If after 3 months of coaching there's no significant change, move to (2) audio-first format shift OR (3) speaker substitution. Don't simply continue giving bad talks; bad talks have public recordings that damage the brand. Meta-lesson: 'practice will fix it' is sometimes wrong. Some performance issues require structured intervention (coaching) or strategic reframing (different format, different speaker). Knowing when to pivot from raw practice to structured intervention is itself a meta-skill.
rubric: Rejects 'just keep practicing' as adequate intervention
Recommends coaching with specific cost
Offers audio-format shift as a fallback
Considers speaker substitution as a third option
Articulates why raw practice without feedback fails
Notes brand damage from continuing bad public talks

teachback.prompt: Explain the major pitfalls of the speaking engagements channel. Cover: (1) pitch-slapping; (2) speaking too early; (3) venue selection by prestige; (4) skipping aftermath; (5) no lead capture or attribution; (6) generic talk topics; (7) inadequate practice; (8) no follow-up after the talk; (9) over-commitment; (10) activity-vs-outcome metrics.
rubric: At least 8 pitfalls named with mechanisms
Preventive measures specified for each
Concrete numbers (80% aftermath value, 60–80 hour prep)
Distinguishes activity metrics from outcome metrics
Names attribution as foundational
Articulates how pitfalls compound to destroy ROI
